speaker: interviewer
text: --ver, tell us how you and your wife managed with this baby, and what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I found out there's going to be the people that worked in the shops, that my baby was-- that Edith, like I mentioned before, somehow, some way, I communicated to her that she should bring the baby, hidden in a rucksack or somehow. She brought him over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did she bring him?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Szkolna. They brought all of them, what was left, to Szkolna. Szkolna, we found out later on, was a branch for Majdanek. The people would go from Majdanek, the SS--

speaker: interviewer
text: How far was Szkolna from Radom?

speaker: interviewee
text: Szkolna from Radom? Radom was about, I would say, two or three miles, on the outskirts.

speaker: interviewer
text: A walking distance.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The walking distance-- let's say you're going downtown, or something like this. Or you're going from here-- it was a distance like from here to downtown. When she brought him there, there were a few kids that came the same way, that had been working hard like myself. They were smarter, or maybe got some more money, or more jewelry or whatever. They got somehow all of them in this small camp. How they got them was terrible. Somehow I organized that she was inside the people that had been accepted, that had been registered to stay in this small camp in Szkolna. I got somehow her name, the name from my brother, who later on ran away, and my sister. There came a German. They got a count together. They've got a certain amount of people they've got to take in this transport. They need 80 people more. And this time all of them had been taken the name to stay and to be alive, you see. [GERMAN] Everybody get out. The good and the bad. And he didn't call by name. He just counted how many people are going to be alive, going to be staying. And the rest is just nothing, just the [NON-ENGLISH]. I was so much-- my mind worked. She'd been put on the bad side by the SS, to go away to be killed. I--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know. We know every-- we had to know. A lot of [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], lots of things we had to know. We had to study. Like now you study how to expedite that building. You've got 80 subcontractors. You've got to know in this time how to expedite, and how to save, your brother and sister and the child. [GERMAN] And it was to the bed. And I'd been left with the child. She'd given to me the child.

speaker: interviewer
text: She brought the child to you in the rucksack.

speaker: interviewee
text: To the workshop. We made [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], baskets of [NON-ENGLISH], damn things-- like-- whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is where you worked.

speaker: interviewee
text: We worked. I took the kid. I'd been out. I took at the last minute the kid and I put it in the trash-- a basket in the trash. You see, if I would go out with the kid, I wouldn't be alive. The kid wouldn't be alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was the baby?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nine months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Big baby.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: Big.

speaker: interviewee
text: Big baby. I put him in the [NON-ENGLISH], in the basket.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: During this time, one thing [NON-ENGLISH], the Ukrainian. This is going on. Hundreds and thousands of people are getting what is going on. He said he found a book. Because later on, when everybody was [NON-ENGLISH], the SS then, if somebody is not hidden there, he found the book.

speaker: interviewer
text: He found what?

speaker: interviewee
text: I noticed a Ukrainian before. I used to go to work with him. He was going. And I say-- his name was Sasha. It's my grandchild's name. I don't know how he got it. I said, I've got some gold. Don't say nothing. I showed him-- keep your mouth shut. And this way, the child was saved.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had some gold.

speaker: interviewee
text: She got less the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Wedding ring.

speaker: interviewee
text: Wedding ring.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Edith had some gold.

speaker: interviewee
text: I told this [NON-ENGLISH], the Ukrainian-- he should keep his mouth shut. Even loud, he doesn't care what is going on, if somebody-- I told him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: He understood. He didn't say nothing. He didn't go out, pick up the child, bring him out, and throw him in the wagon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then was happened? When everybody-- most of the people that were left-- there was a place called [NON-ENGLISH]. They killed them-- they killed them all off. She would be what was going. And some of them-- the children were seven or six. And they were going with their mothers over there. Still there were left three children, including mine. Everybody got a different-- like you said--

words.text: She would be what was going.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now how did you get the baby out of the trash?

speaker: interviewee
text: After they came back in--

speaker: interviewer
text: You went out for this selection.

speaker: interviewee
text: After the selection, everybody went back to his job, to his place where he's going to sleep and the place where he's going to eat. We went back. We picked up the child.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was the baby in the trash?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I would say-- about three hours? Four hours? Two hours?

speaker: interviewer
text: And was he OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was OK. Because she gave him sleeping pills. She always lets me hide it. We got sleeping pills to give him. And he was sleeping.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did she get the sleeping pills?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything's got a story. It would take another-- she had a friend. He was a policeman. And he used to go in the ghetto-- the small ghetto, the small [NON-ENGLISH]. Used to go out. He was able to go out to the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. How do you say that?

speaker: interviewer
text: Pharmacy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pharmacy. And she got them.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So you got back to-- you were with Edith now. And you got back. And you got your baby. Then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then what happened? Now the tragedy starts-- a tragedy. The leadership in the camp-- we had a Jewish doctor. They got the child-- it was legal, [GERMAN]. It was legal. It was nine years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: He came in. He said, what is it going to be? You cannot. You cannot have a child here. Take a look. People are--

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], Mr. Doctor, what am I going to do? I cannot help it. I've got the child. I'm not going to do what the Germans are doing.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, well, you saw people that are 20, 30 years old--

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: They've been killed. It's going to take only a few minutes. He called in the sister with the organi-- we give him a small injection. It takes very easy. A few minutes, and he's going to be finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, what-- to this policeman-- the Yiddish policeman, the Jewish policeman-- and the Jewish doctor-- you [NON-ENGLISH]. The guys haven't killed him. You're going to kill him?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's going to live. And I know he was afraid. And child's going to be maybe-- and I took him. And I threw them on the [NON-ENGLISH]. I threw them-- [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK. We have translators. You can say it in Polish or Yiddish. It's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: My child is going to live. And your child is going to die. You don't want that. What we've done, after a couple of days, it was the same thing. After a day later, the German [NON-ENGLISH] came. And they came to inspection for this camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they found three children. They found only one. And Edith-- they make a appell. They called, everybody [GERMAN]. Where is the mother of this child? I hold Edith. You're going to die? The child's going to die. And what good is it going to do? And she didn't listen to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this time they were together. Because it was a time for the-- and normally the women were separated and men were separated.

speaker: interviewer
text: She ran out to this guy and said, I'm the mother to this child. And he asked her, how old is this child? She told him, nine months.

speaker: interviewee
text: He's 11. It's the reason she always remember 11. He's 11 months old or 12 months old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he asked her, you think this child's got the possibility to be alive? The SS will murder it. He's got a possibility?

speaker: interviewee
text: She found a story. And the Germans believed. I have two sisters. But they're in a different camp. We're going to transport him over there. He knows it's impossible to transport, to go to-- you can go over there, take him. Oh, he believed her. He said, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. This child must disappear. He hadn't killed him. And he let her free and him free. Now I come back to this guy, to this doctor with this nurse--

speaker: interviewer
text: So somehow Edith talked him out of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Somehow she talked him out. And he believed her. This German-- he's a killer. She talked her way-- and he believed it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then what happened was the leadership in the new camp, in Szkolna, came in. And they said we have to get rid of the child. I said like before. I repeated myself. It's possible. We give him an injection. And they--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what happened I told already, about this guy with this doctor, with this nurse.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you save your baby?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, give me time. Next, what we did, in this same-- not far away-- my brother was working, I told you, with this Jewish-- one side was Gentile, and the other side was concentration camp. He was in touch before I got it. Someday she'd be able-- Marisha, this Gentile woman. She'd be able to take this child. Somehow we'd get him out.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you name your baby?

speaker: interviewee
text: In this time, we give him another name. She gave him the name Henry, a Polish name. And we wrote a note in Polish. We asked, good Gentile people-- not Gentile. Good people, we have marriage problems-- whatever, in this note. Before this, we-- let me remember. It was when he told us-- we asked the doctor, the leadership, to give me a day. I will somehow arrange to get the baby out from Szkolna. We'll take him out. And you don't tell him what you're going to do. Somehow my brother got in touch with Marisha through this guy.

words.text: She gave him the name Henry,

speaker: interviewer
text: So is Marisha going to take this baby?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Marisha got already my brother's baby-- a grown-up girl. She raised her. They took her on the estate, the SS. The Polish people showed she Jewish-- finally, she got him out. And she's now got grandchildren herself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was Marisha helping you?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a friend of my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: It wasn't a question of bribery?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. You couldn't help with bribery over there. She was a friend. He had lived with her. He [NON-ENGLISH] talk talk a lot of stories. He went to Israel. And he say, why--

speaker: interviewer
text: But right now, I want you to tell me how the baby survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: How the baby survived-- well, I got the baby out. We haven't got facilities to wash yourself or to take a shower or something. They brought us groupwise to Jewish-- to city showers. Through this, I put him again in a rucksack. I put him to sleep. I took him out--

speaker: interviewer
text: With the sleeping pills again?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same thing. And we went to the shower. We took him with us a day later. We couldn't keep him over a day. And we brought him to a place-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Jews used to live there. [NON-ENGLISH]. He was living whole girlfriends, [NON-ENGLISH]. A girlfriend of Marisha was living there. And she told-- [NON-ENGLISH]. She hadn't got children. She's going to somehow convince her that she should take the baby. She should put the baby--

speaker: interviewer
text: Marisha would convince her friend.

speaker: interviewee
text: Her friend. She should take the baby. What we did is we took the baby, with a note. We didn't tell her it's a Jewish baby. With a note we put the baby under her door. And she picked up the baby-- Marisha was outside. She could go. Because she's Gentile, she could be outside. She picked up the baby and haven't walked us, like [INAUDIBLE] each other, going to take the baby. She called the Polish police. And they picked up the baby. And they gave the baby to a Polish orphanage.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the baby circumcised?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We circumcised him in Baltimore when he was seven years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: That saved his life.

speaker: interviewee
text: How we got this is a separate story. How we got-- in Sinai Hospital in Baltimore they wouldn't let our mohel [NON-ENGLISH]. It's a story. I have to tell you a story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Later. But later. So right now, this uncircumcised baby, who looked like any other baby, then--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So finally I--

speaker: interviewer
text: So he's in an orphanage.

speaker: interviewee
text: Edith couldn't make peace with herself. She'd done the same thing-- [INAUDIBLE] been like Majdanek, like Buchenwald, like Auschwitz. You could somehow communicate with outside. You could take off this band and go outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Jewish star.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she went to this orphanage. She found out which orphanage. Not directly. She took a [INAUDIBLE]. And this time, she got some candies, cookies, and she brought-- she found us. She made peace with herself.

speaker: interviewer
text: She went to this orphanage. And she found the baby. And then what did she do?

speaker: interviewee
text: She saw already. She came back. And that's it. At least she knew he was alive. Whatever's going to happen is going to happen. He never would come back to us. Because the Poles have a law. If the original parents don't come back, they haven't got the right for this child. Because this child later on has been adopted, and Polish. It's a whole story.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. But for this time, you knew the child was at least safe someplace.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened to you and Edith?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then what happened? Later we went to the right things, to Majdanek. Ever heard of Majdanek?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get to Majdanek?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took-- I mentioned before. Majdanek-- Szkolna was a branch of Majdanek. How'd they get there-- Still they always found elderly people, or people who were not productive, or didn't like it. They need some people. They took them to Majdanek.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the same thing you see in the movies, the wagons. They transported us.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went on a train.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the train--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: You saw it. I cannot describe it to you. You saw it already in the movies. You saw--

speaker: interviewer
text: But I wanted you to tell me. How many people were in this wagon?

speaker: interviewee
text: In this group which left Radom, which departed from Radom, I would say it was maybe about 400 or 500.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many in the car with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the car must have been-- they brought in lot and other communities together. They made-- I don't know-- maybe transported 1,000, 2,000, 3,000.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many were with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Radom, when we went to Majdanek, it was about 300. We came out in Majdanek with maybe 50 or 60.

speaker: interviewer
text: All the rest died?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the rest died from the hard work or been killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: But on the trip-- let's go. From Radom, you went to Majdanek on the train, crowded in, terrible. You want to describe it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, crowded in. We decide-- we saw this. We came to Majdanek. We don't want to go like all of them were going, and be dead like to the crematorium. We'd like to go on the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. How do you say the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]? On the wire.

speaker: interviewer
text: You want to kill yourself against the fence?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kill ourselves. We want to kill ourselves, because a lot had done this. And finally, I don't know. Something happened. We gave up the idea of killing ourselves.

words.text: because a lot had done this.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take you to get from Radom to Majdanek on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: It took a long time. It took maybe two days or three days or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any sanitation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any sanitation on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Everything goes together. Terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any food on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Nothing. The first food we got was in Majdanek. They gave us a piece of bread, whatever. Who remembers? Over there--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when they opened the door at Majdanek?

speaker: interviewee
text: They opened. Some of us were dead. The stronger, the younger, was alive. Some of them because--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see some people dead on that train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. You saw-- you've been in the wagon with them that were dead. Sick-- there was a kid who had been through the selection. A very rich Jew, the kid was a cripple. And he couldn't survive. Nobody--

words.text: there was a kid who had

speaker: interviewer
text: When they opened the door, what did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I saw-- they took us to quarantine, they called it at this time. And the lagerfuhrer was Polish [NON-ENGLISH]. He said in Polish, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. You see-- right away, we hadn't experienced a crematorium yet. And over there-- he was nasty. He tried to-- he told us this would happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: He showed you the smokestack?

speaker: interviewee
text: The smokestack-- yeah. You go up the-- it's coming. He'd say-- how do you say what the smoke goes through?

speaker: interviewer
text: Smokestack.

speaker: interviewee
text: Smoke-- well--

speaker: interviewer
text: Chimney.

speaker: interviewee
text: Chimney. That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see German soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too many German soldiers. The Germans used the Poles to do the dirty work. Let's say in 20,000 whatever you call them, it used to be let's say 20 or 30 Germans. The officers were the main thing there, showing them what to do. And the dirty work, it was Ukrainians and Poles that did the dirty work. Now--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you're at Majdanek.

speaker: interviewee
text: At Majdanek, we somehow went back to the same work that we were doing in Szkolna.

speaker: interviewer
text: First were you assigned to a barrack?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. First the barrack. But this was not a barrack. [NON-ENGLISH], field. Over there, before, was a camp with 25,000 30,000 people. Two days before, they killed them. 20,000. Before we came [INAUDIBLE] they knew of the Russian approach already, closer to Poland. They killed 22,000 people in one day.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: What year? This must be '43.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what month? Was it cold or warm?

speaker: interviewee
text: What month-- we left-- it must be around '43. It must be around February or March.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was cold.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, cold.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they give you a number when you got off the train at Majdanek?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did they put the number-- where did they put it?

speaker: interviewee
text: They put it in a [NON-ENGLISH], a red-- political was red. Murder was so--

speaker: interviewer
text: What color was yours?

speaker: interviewee
text: Red. It was political. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. Political [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] is a murderer. Over there--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Edith with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything-- Edith was with me. She was a different field. In the 22,000 Jews, I found a friend of my friend. He knows he's not going to-- because they took him to bury, to clean up after the dead. He gave me a razor blade. It was a big thing-- like a Gillette. He gave me a piece of soap. It was a big thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. He was from Rokitna. He was not-- he married a girl from Radom.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he helped you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I met him. He said, well, we will go. They'd been left, the [NON-ENGLISH], the people that cleaned up. They saw the whole business of the 22,000 Jews. He saw this thing. He said, I am not going to be alive. [NON-ENGLISH] some people tried to get us through-- many different ways. Ridiculous. You've got to really be able to put yourself in a place where you take out the trash, to hide under the-- somehow. Most were caught. I remember, they made the [NON-ENGLISH] The commander people had been with us maybe, I don't know, a couple weeks. And they disappeared. You know, they got an A-- [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. Jews that did the work to finish.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, stop.

speaker: interviewee
text: After this--

speaker: interviewer
text: Stop.
